title: Digestive upset support for dogs and cats
summary: Mild stomach upset should be watched closely, with attention to hydration, appetite, stool quality, and changes in energy level.
care_steps: Watch water intake and note whether vomiting or diarrhea keeps happening.
Offer a quiet rest period and avoid introducing new treats or foods while symptoms are active.
Write down when the symptoms started and any recent food, medication, or routine changes.
Call your veterinarian if the digestive signs continue or your pet becomes weak or uncomfortable.

suggested_tasks.title: Monitor food and water intake
suggested_tasks.rationale: Digestive issues need close hydration and appetite tracking.

suggested_tasks.title: Log vomiting or stool changes
suggested_tasks.rationale: A symptom log helps the vet understand pattern and severity.

title: Ear discomfort and infection warning signs
summary: Ear problems often show up as scratching, odor, discharge, pain, or head shaking, and many cases need veterinary treatment rather than home experimentation.
care_steps: Watch for discharge, odor, swelling, repeated scratching, or head shaking.
Do not use new ear cleaners or medications unless they were already approved by your veterinarian.
Prevent additional scratching or rubbing that could worsen the irritation.
Schedule a veterinary exam if pain, odor, or swelling continues.

suggested_tasks.title: Check ears for odor or discharge
suggested_tasks.rationale: Consistent checks help you notice worsening inflammation quickly.

suggested_tasks.title: Schedule ear exam with vet
suggested_tasks.rationale: Persistent ear pain usually needs vet-directed treatment.

title: Skin irritation and coat care basics
summary: Skin irritation can come from moisture, allergies, infection, or parasites, so the safest first step is careful monitoring plus a clean, low-irritation environment.
care_steps: Check the skin for redness, odor, open sores, crusting, or moist irritated spots.
Keep bedding and high-contact areas clean and dry.
Avoid applying new topical products unless they are already vet-approved for your pet.
Book a veterinary visit if the rash spreads, smells bad, or your pet is damaging the skin by scratching.

suggested_tasks.title: Inspect irritated skin areas
suggested_tasks.rationale: Daily checks show whether redness or swelling is improving.

suggested_tasks.title: Wash pet bedding
suggested_tasks.rationale: Clean bedding reduces irritants and helps skin recovery.

title: Parasite prevention and follow-up
summary: External and internal parasites can affect skin, stool, appetite, and energy, and prevention only works well when the full household plan is followed consistently.
care_steps: Check the coat, ears, bedding, and stool for fleas, flea dirt, ticks, or visible worms.
Use only vet-approved parasite prevention products labeled for your pet's species and size.
Wash bedding and clean common resting areas if parasites are suspected.
Contact your veterinarian if your pet looks weak, pale, or keeps losing weight.

suggested_tasks.title: Inspect stool and coat for parasites
suggested_tasks.rationale: Direct observation helps confirm what the vet needs to know.

suggested_tasks.title: Wash bedding and vacuum pet areas

title: Mobility support and pain monitoring
summary: Mobility changes often need reduced activity, careful pain monitoring, and prompt veterinary assessment when movement suddenly worsens.
care_steps: Limit jumping, rough play, stairs, and slippery surfaces until the issue is understood.
Provide a padded resting area that is easy to reach.
Track when the limping or weakness is worst and whether it is getting better or worse.
Contact your veterinarian promptly if your pet cannot bear weight or seems very painful.

suggested_tasks.title: Set up padded rest area
suggested_tasks.rationale: Mobility issues improve when movement demands are reduced.

suggested_tasks.title: Track limping or pain changes
suggested_tasks.rationale: Trend notes help the vet judge severity and progression.

title: General urgent-care warning signs
summary: Serious changes in breathing, pain, bleeding, swelling, or responsiveness should be treated as urgent and should not wait for routine monitoring alone.
care_steps: If symptoms are severe or rapidly worsening, call your veterinary clinic immediately.
Bring a short symptom timeline, medication list, and food history if you need same-day care.
Avoid giving human medications unless your veterinarian has explicitly prescribed them for this situation.

suggested_tasks.title: Prepare symptom summary for vet
suggested_tasks.rationale: A short symptom summary speeds up urgent care communication.
